question: Using Table 5.2-5, compare the chloride and fluoride limits with the dissolved oxygen limit in primary coolant.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s compliance with regulatory requirements for overpressure protection, specifying the use of ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases as endorsed by the NRC and referenced in relevant regulations and regulatory guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, inspection, and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC conditions, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards relevant to the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases related to materials, quality assurance, and inspection procedures for pressurized water reactors.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details that these components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III, 2013 Edition standards, and clarifies the classification of various piping and components according to their safety significance.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and meet the regulatory requirements of 10 CFR 50, including General Design Criteria for quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture prevention. The materials for Class 1 components, such as the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and steam generators (SGs), are specified in Table 5.2-4, ensuring compliance with applicable material and testing standards.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and supports key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to more detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the relevant figures and tables that define safe operating conditions to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor vessel.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the inspection, shipment, installation, operating conditions, inservice surveillance, and threaded fastener requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards. It specifies procedures for maintaining vessel integrity during transport and operation, and lists the key codes and standards used for compliance.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel contains two integral steam generators (SGs) that are part of the reactor coolant system. The steam generator system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the reactor pressure vessel itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs. The design ensures separation between the primary and secondary sides, with the steam generator tubes and associated welds forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to meet safety and performance requirements during normal and off-normal conditions.

edges.description: Section A discusses compliance with regulatory codes for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B lists references including water chemistry guidelines that likely specify limits for chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen, thus Section A's compliance is supported by the standards referenced in Section B.

Section A references specific ASME code provisions and testing programs that are detailed in Section B's broader statement of compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory codes for overpressure protection, while Section B specifies that the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials also comply with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, thus both sections are connected through their adherence to the same regulatory and code standards.

Section A lists references, including EPRI chemistry guidelines, that are used to establish the technical basis for primary coolant chemistry limits, while Section B asserts compliance with regulatory codes (such as ASME BPVC) that require adherence to such chemistry limits, thus connecting the referenced guidelines in A as supporting evidence for the compliance claims in B.

Section A provides references to industry standards and guidelines that are used to establish the chemical limits (such as chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen) relevant to the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials described in Section B.

Section A states that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC Section III, while Section B specifies that the RCPB materials themselves conform to the fabrication, construction, and material requirements of the same ASME BPVC sections, thus Section B provides detailed material compliance supporting the general compliance claim in Section A.

Section A describes operational procedures and water chemistry management relevant to primary coolant, while Section B outlines regulatory code compliance for reactor coolant boundary integrity, connecting them through the requirement that water chemistry (including chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen limits) must satisfy the regulatory standards for system integrity.

Section A describes operational procedures and water chemistry management relevant to system components, while Section B establishes that these components must comply with regulatory codes (such as ASME BPVC) which include water chemistry limits like those for chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen, thus connecting operational practices to regulatory requirements.

Section A discusses mechanical and examination requirements for reactor vessel attachments, while Section B addresses overpressure protection and code compliance for the reactor coolant boundary; both reference ASME BPVC standards, connecting them through shared regulatory design and inspection codes.

Section A discusses mechanical and examination requirements for reactor vessel components, while Section B lists references including water chemistry guidelines that would contain the chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen limits mentioned in the analysis question, thus Section A would reference or rely on the standards in Section B for chemical limits.

Section A discusses specific ASME BPVC code requirements for welds and attachments on the reactor vessel, while Section B states that the reactor coolant pressure boundary components comply with 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC Section III, thus Section A provides details/specifications for the general compliance described in Section B.

Section A and Section B both reference compliance with ASME BPVC Section III and related regulatory requirements for reactor vessel and coolant boundary integrity, establishing a shared regulatory framework for material and operational limits such as those for chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen.

Section A and Section B both reference ASME NQA-1-2008/1a-2009 Addenda as a quality assurance standard for reactor vessel and coolant boundary integrity, establishing a shared regulatory basis for material and environmental controls relevant to coolant chemistry limits.

Section A describes specific inspection and handling requirements for the reactor vessel that reference compliance with ASME BPVC and NQA-1 standards, while Section B establishes that the reactor coolant boundary components, including the reactor vessel, are designed and constructed to meet 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC requirements, thus Section A's practices are implemented to satisfy the broader regulatory compliance stated in Section B.
Section B references the use of a non-chloride environment for the reactor vessel, which is related to the chloride limits mentioned in Table 5.2-5 in Section A's context of water chemistry control.

Section A discusses weld examination and attachment requirements per ASME code, while Section B outlines inspection, shipment, and storage requirements for the reactor vessel, referencing ASME standards and specifying a non-chloride environment for RPV surfaces; both sections are connected through their reliance on ASME BPVC and NQA-1 standards for reactor vessel integrity and handling.

Section A describes the components and design of the reactor coolant system, which is the context for the chemical limits in Table 5.2-5, while Section B addresses regulatory code compliance for the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, which underpins the need for such chemical limits to protect system integrity.

Section A describes the components of the reactor coolant system, including steam generators, while Section B lists references that provide the regulatory and technical basis for primary water chemistry limits such as chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen.
Section A describes the components and design basis of the reactor coolant system, which are subject to the regulatory requirements for integrity and material compatibility specified in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design and function, while Section B details the regulatory codes and standards (such as ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a) that the system must comply with, connecting the system's design to its regulatory requirements.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system, while Section B lists references, including EPRI chemistry guidelines, that specify regulatory limits for primary coolant impurities such as chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen, thus connecting the system description to the applicable chemical limits.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system and its components, while Section B establishes that these components must comply with regulatory codes (such as ASME BPVC), which include limits on coolant chemistry parameters like chloride, fluoride, and dissolved oxygen.

Section A provides specific details about the components within the reactor coolant system, including steam generators, while Section B gives a general overview of the reactor coolant system and its subsystems, establishing the context for the components listed in Section A.
